brief_document.title: aion-client (Rust): crate scaffold, connection, core operations, and error taxonomy
blocked_by: AW: aion-proto crate must be published in the workspace
brief_document.purpose: Build the foundation of the Rust caller SDK: a crate that connects to an aion-server, exposes the unary operations (start, signal, query, cancel, list, describe) over the transport, and maps every server failure to a branchable ClientError. This is the network client every Rust application uses to drive workflows, and the reference against which the other-language SDKs are compared.
brief_document.task: Create the aion-client crate as a workspace member depending on aion-proto and aion-core (and external crates), never on aion/aion-store/beamr by default. Implement ClientBuilder/Client for connection with auth + TLS, a transport module mapping the six unary operations onto the aion-proto request/response types, and the ClientError taxonomy from the AL-001 contract. The WorkflowHandle, typed-payload helpers, event Stream, and embedded binding land in AL-003 — this brief returns bare WorkflowId/RunId from start and uses raw Payload arguments.

requirements.title: Crate scaffold and workspace wiring
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL create the aion-client crate as a workspace member whose lib.rs contains only declarations and re-exports. THE SYSTEM SHALL depend on aion-proto and aion-core plus external crates, and SHALL NOT depend on aion, aion-store, or beamr by default.
acceptance: crates/aion-client exists and is registered in the workspace Cargo.toml members list.
Cargo.toml declares aion-proto and aion-core as the only aion-* dependencies; aion is absent or gated behind an off-by-default feature reserved for AL-003.
src/lib.rs contains only pub mod declarations and pub use re-exports, no logic.
The crate sets unsafe_code = "deny" and inherits the workspace clippy lints.
cargo check -p aion-client passes.

requirements.title: ClientError taxonomy
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL define ClientError as a closed enum covering the ten variants from the AL-001 contract, implemented with thiserror. IF a server condition matches a known variant, THEN THE SYSTEM SHALL map it to that variant; IF it is an unexpected server error, THEN THE SYSTEM SHALL map it to Server carrying the server's detail. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT collapse distinct conditions into one variant.
acceptance: error.rs defines ClientError with variants NotFound, AlreadyExists, QueryFailed, QueryTimeout, Cancelled, Unavailable, Unauthenticated, NamespaceDenied { detail: String }, InvalidArgument, and Server { detail: String }.
ClientError derives Debug, implements Display and std::error::Error via thiserror, and is Send + Sync + 'static.

A mapping function converts an aion-proto / transport error into the correct ClientError variant, with a unit test asserting at least the NotFound, AlreadyExists, QueryTimeout, and Unavailable mappings.
No mapping path returns a generic Server for a condition that has a specific variant.

requirements.title: ClientBuilder, Client, and transport connection
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL provide a ClientBuilder that connects to an aion-server endpoint with an auth credential and TLS configuration and produces a reusable Client. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT require re-connection per operation.
acceptance: client.rs defines Client and ClientBuilder; ClientBuilder::new(endpoint) plus with_auth(...) and with_tls(...) configure the connection and build() yields a Client (async).
transport.rs holds the network transport over aion-proto; Client holds a cheaply-cloneable transport handle reused across operations.
Connecting with a bad endpoint or failed handshake yields ClientError::Unavailable; a rejected credential yields ClientError::Unauthenticated.
Client is Clone and Send + Sync so it can be shared across tasks.
A doc example shows constructing a Client from an endpoint and credential.

requirements.title: Unary operations: start, signal, query, cancel, list, describe
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL implement start, signal, query, cancel, list, and describe on Client over the transport, each returning Result<_, ClientError>, mapping inputs and outputs to the aion-proto types. THE SYSTEM SHALL surface a query deadline as QueryTimeout and SHALL NOT treat cancel as a confirmation that the workflow has stopped.
acceptance: ops.rs implements start(workflow_type, input: Payload, opts) -> Result<(WorkflowId, RunId), ClientError>, signal(&WorkflowId, run, name, payload: Payload), query(&WorkflowId, run, name, args: Payload, deadline) -> Result<Payload, ClientError>, cancel(&WorkflowId, run, reason), list(&WorkflowFilter, page) -> Result<Vec<WorkflowSummary>, ClientError>, and describe(&WorkflowId, run) -> Result<WorkflowDescription, ClientError>.
Operations target the latest run by default and a specific RunId when the run argument is Some, per the AL-001 contract.
query enforces the supplied deadline and returns ClientError::QueryTimeout when it elapses; a handler error returns QueryFailed.
Operating on an unknown workflow returns ClientError::NotFound.
WorkflowSummary and WorkflowDescription reuse aion-core types where they exist (status from aion-core::WorkflowStatus); list pagination matches the contract.
Each operation has a unit test against a stub transport asserting the request mapping and the error mapping for at least one failure case.

boundaries: SHALL NOT define the WorkflowHandle type, typed serde payload helpers, the event Stream, or the embedded binding — those land in AL-003.
SHALL NOT define aion-proto wire types or aion-server endpoints — it consumes them (AW).
SHALL NOT depend on aion, aion-store, or beamr by default.
verification: No #[allow], #[expect], or #[ignore] directives in new code.
cargo tree -p aion-client shows no dependency on aion, aion-store, or beamr.

checklist.text: ClientBuilder connects to an aion-server endpoint with auth credential and TLS configuration and produces a reusable Client; the Client depends on aion-proto and aion-core, not on aion/aion-store/beamr by default.
Client exposes start, signal, query, cancel, list, and describe over the network transport, returning idiomatic Result<_, ClientError>.

ClientError is a closed enum covering NotFound, AlreadyExists, QueryFailed, QueryTimeout, Cancelled, Unavailable, Unauthenticated, InvalidArgument, and Server, implementing std::error::Error via thiserror.

stories.text: As an application developer, I want to add a client dependency, point it at a server URL, and start a workflow by type name with a typed input so that I can trigger durable work from my service without learning the engine internals.
As an operator, I want to list workflows by type, status, and time range so that I can see what is running and what has failed.

As an operator, I want to cancel a run with a reason and understand that it is a cooperative request, not an immediate kill, so that I do not assume the workflow stopped the instant the call returned.

As a platform team, I want each client to map server failures to a documented, branchable error taxonomy so that my code can react differently to an idempotency conflict, a query timeout, and an auth failure.

constraints.text: Clients consume the aion-server API and aion-proto wire types; they SHALL NOT define their own wire formats, endpoints, or fields the server does not serve. The protocol is owned by AW.

Clients SHALL NOT depend on engine internals (aion, aion-store, beamr). The Rust client may depend on aion-core (shared domain types) and aion-proto; the embedded constructor may depend on aion only behind a feature flag, never by default.
All four SDKs expose the same seven operations with identical observable behaviour, verified by the shared conformance suite.
Every SDK exposes both a typed payload surface (per language) and a raw-Payload escape hatch; neither is removable.
Every SDK maps server failures to a documented, branchable error taxonomy. No swallowed errors, no single catch-all collapsing distinct failures.

subscribe resumes transparently across transient disconnects using the per-event sequence number, delivering a gap-free duplicate-free stream; terminal failures surface through the stream, not as a silent end.
start honours a caller-supplied idempotency key: a retried identical start returns the original handle; a conflicting reuse raises AlreadyExists.

Per-ecosystem toolchain gates pass: Rust (clippy -D warnings, fmt --check, no allow/expect/ignore), Python (ruff + mypy --strict + pytest), TypeScript (tsc strict + eslint + tests), Gleam (gleam format --check + gleam check + gleam test). No-partial-implementation and no-silent-failure standards hold across all four.
Each package is publishable to its registry (crates.io, PyPI, npm, Hex) with a README, a type-checked public surface, and a runnable example covering all seven operations.

resolved_context.intention: Ship the caller-side SDKs for the workflow-caller role: aion-client (Rust, crates.io), aion-client-python (PyPI), aion-client-typescript (npm), and aion_client (Gleam, Hex). Each connects to an aion-server deployment and exposes the same seven operations — connect, start, signal, query, cancel, list, describe, subscribe — in the idioms of its language. A developer who has never read the engine internals should add a dependency, point at a server URL, and start a workflow with full type-checking in their language within minutes; a frontend developer using the TypeScript client should never see beamr, Gleam, or Rust. The four SDKs must behave identically where behaviour is observable, proven by a shared language-neutral behavioural contract and a conformance suite that exercises every SDK against a real server.
